abstract: Antibiotic-associated diarrhoea is a frequent complication of systemic antibacterial therapy, often resulting in treatment discontinuation, extended hospital stays, and increased healthcare costs. While probiotics have been proposed to mitigate antibiotic-induced gut dysbiosis, definitive clinical evidence regarding their prophylactic efficacy in general adult populations remains inconsistently established. To address this, a multicentre, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial was conducted to evaluate the efficacy of multi-strain probiotic co-administration in preventing antibiotic-associated diarrhoea. Adult patients aged 18 years and older who were prescribed broad-spectrum systemic antibiotics for a duration of five to fourteen days were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to receive either a daily high-dose probiotic formulation or a visually identical placebo. The intervention commenced within twenty-four hours of antibiotic initiation and continued for seven days following the completion of the antibiotic course. The primary clinical outcome was the incidence of antibiotic-associated diarrhoea, defined a priori as three or more unformed stools per day for at least two consecutive days within thirty days of antibiotic cessation. A total of 842 participants were randomized and included in the intention-to-treat analysis, with 421 allocated to each group. Baseline demographic and clinical characteristics were well balanced between the cohorts. The incidence of antibiotic-associated diarrhoea was significantly reduced in the probiotic group, occurring in 36 of 421 patients (8.6%), compared to 64 of 421 patients (15.2%) in the placebo group. This represented a relative risk of 0.56 (95% confidence interval, 0.38 to 0.83; p = 0.004). The incidence of adverse events was low and did not differ significantly between the two treatment groups. In conclusion, the concurrent administration of a multi-strain probiotic with systemic antibiotics is a safe and effective strategy for reducing the incidence of antibiotic-associated diarrhoea in adult patients.

This randomized, sham-controlled clinical trial aimed to evaluate the efficacy of arthroscopic surgery compared with a sham procedure in reducing pain and improving physical function in adults with symptomatic knee osteoarthritis. A total of 320 adults with moderate to severe osteoarthritis were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to undergo either arthroscopic debridement and lavage or a sham surgical procedure comprising superficial skin incisions only. The primary outcomes were self-reported knee pain and physical function, measured using the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index (WOMAC) on a scale from 0 to 100, with higher scores indicating worse symptoms. Outcomes were assessed at multiple time points up to 24 months post-intervention. At baseline, demographic and clinical characteristics were well-balanced between the two groups. Over the 24-month follow-up period, both groups experienced moderate improvements in pain and function; however, there were no statistically significant or clinically meaningful differences between the intervention and comparator groups. At the primary 12-month endpoint, the mean between-group difference in WOMAC pain scores was -1.4 points (95% confidence interval [CI], -3.8 to 1.0; p = 0.25). Similarly, the mean difference in WOMAC physical function scores at 12 months was -2.1 points (95% CI, -5.3 to 1.1; p = 0.20). The incidence of adverse events was comparable between the surgical and sham cohorts. These findings demonstrate that arthroscopic debridement and lavage provide no additional benefit for symptom relief or functional improvement compared to a sham surgical procedure. Consequently, the routine use of arthroscopic surgery for the management of knee osteoarthritis is not supported by these data.
